hook: One of the world's first psychiatric hospitals, founded 1286, where music and water treated the mad.
facts: Founded by the Marinid sultan Abu Ya'qub Yusuf in 1286, between Tala'a Kebira and the Zaouia of Moulay Idriss II.
Functioned as a hospice for the destitute and mentally ill until 1944, one of its kind's longest runs.
Treatment used Andalusian music, aromas and running water; Leo Africanus worked here in its decline.
Now rebuilt as a funduq-style building of shops; restoration to a medical use has been proposed.
legend: Said to have been a model for Europe's first asylum, founded in Valencia in 1410.
w: A Marinid sultan founded this in 1286 to house the city's destitute and mad, treating them with running water, sweet aromas and Andalusian music drifting through the wards. Leo Africanus saw it already decaying. It limped on until 1944, then became a row of shops. Medieval psychiatry, walled in commerce.

hook: A shaded little square of herbalists and blue pottery, set around the old psychiatric hospital.
facts: Small medina square shaded by old plane trees, beside the rebuilt Maristan of Sidi Frej.
Appeared after the 16th century, first as a market for earthenware pots.
Stalls sell henna, kohl, argan oil, black soap and hammam goods alongside blue Fassi ceramics.
Around half the shops are cosmetic herbalists, some barely three metres square.
w: Two plane trees shade a pocket square where herbalists weigh out henna, kohl and black soap for the hammam, and stacks of blue Fassi pottery climb the walls. Shops no bigger than a cupboard. At its edge, a plaque to the medieval madhouse it grew up around. The medina at half-speed, leaves rustling overhead.

hook: A Marinid college named for the reflecting pool at its heart, with some of Morocco's earliest zellij.
facts: Commissioned in 1321 by the Marinid sultan Abu al-Hassan, near the Mosque of the Andalusians.
Named for the sahrij, the large rectangular water basin filling its courtyard.
Holds some of the earliest zellij mosaic tilework found in any Moroccan madrasa.
Restored by heritage agency ADER-Fes and reopened to visitors in 2017.
w: Across the river in the Andalusian quarter, far from the madrasa crowds, a still rectangular pool fills the courtyard and gives the place its name. Carved cedar, a screen of early zellij, the slosh of water against tile. Built 1321, shut for years, reopened in 2017. The quiet twin of the famous colleges.

hook: The slipper-makers' mosque, its Marinid minaret one of the finest on the main medina street.
facts: Built in the 14th century, likely by the Marinid sultan Abu al-Hasan between 1331 and 1348.
Named for the cherbil, an embroidered women's slipper sold in the surrounding shops.
Minaret and entrance keep their Marinid form; the rest was rebuilt under Moulay Slimane.
Minaret carries fine darj-w-ktaf sebka patterning, polylobed arches and zellij infill.
w: Halfway down Tala'a Kebira, where shops still sell the pointed embroidered slippers called cherbil, a slim Marinid minaret rises in carved palmette netting and coloured tile. The body of the mosque was redone two centuries back, but this tower and its doorway have stood since the 1300s. Named for cobblers.

hook: One of the city's oldest merchant caravanserais, built for traders from Tetouan in the north.
facts: Founded in the 14th century under the Marinids, around the time of the al-Attarine Madrasa.
Name means 'people of Tetouan'; merchants in Fes were grouped by their city of origin.
Functioned purely as a merchants' inn, with rooms above a galleried courtyard.
Among the oldest surviving funduqs in Fes el-Bali.
w: Traders down from Tetouan slept here above their goods, in a galleried courtyard built for merchants who lodged with their own kind from their own town. Six hundred years on it still stands, one of the oldest inns in the medina, near the perfumers' madrasa. A caravanserai with a postcode and a hometown.

hook: The goldsmiths' inn of 1711, an Alaouite-era trading court near the coppersmiths' square.
facts: Built in 1711 under the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail, named 'inn of the goldsmiths'.
Likely raised by powerful local merchants or officials as a commercial hub.
Became one of the most important funduqs in Fes, a centre of merchant dealing.
Stands in the heart of Fes el-Bali near Place Seffarine.
w: Built in 1711 for the goldsmiths' trade, this courtyard inn near the hammering of the coppersmiths once ran on the dealings of the medina's richest merchants. Tiers of rooms ring a worn central court. Moulay Ismail's century, in stone and cedar. A stock exchange you could sleep in.

hook: A walled-off citadel quarter of Saharan settlers, self-governed for centuries by twelve elders.
facts: Name means 'citadel of the flowers'; also called Kasbah Filala for its Tafilalt settlers.
First raised in the Almohad period, taking present form under the early Alaouites.
Sealed by its own walls, entered only through the towered gate Bab Chorfa off Place Baghdadi.
Self-governed by a council of twelve elected elders managing its own waqf revenues.
w: A whole quarter behind its own walls, settled by followers of the Alaouite sultans from the desert oases of the Tafilalt, with one way in through a towered gate off Place Baghdadi. For centuries twelve elected elders ran it, raising taxes, fixing drains, answering to no governor. 'Citadel of the flowers', shut tight.

hook: The farriers' gate into Fes el-Jdid, once the threshold of the Jewish Mellah.
facts: Monumental southern gate of Fes el-Jdid, part of the 1276 Marinid foundation of the new city.
Name, 'Gate of the Farriers', recalls the horseshoe smiths who worked nearby.
Originally called Bab Oyun Sanhaja, 'gate of the water sources of the Sanhaja'.
Once had a bent defensive entrance, straightened for traffic in the 20th century.
w: Horseshoe smiths once rang their hammers under this Marinid arch, the southern way into the new royal city of 1276. Beyond it spread the Mellah, the Jewish quarter walled into Fes el-Jdid. The bent entrance built to baffle attackers has been straightened for carts and crowds. The farriers' gate, still busy.

hook: The towering ceremonial gate of the Mechouar, stage for royal parades and grisly displays.
facts: Tall fortified gate between the Old and New Mechouar, dating from the 1276 Marinid foundation.
Also known as Bab Sbaa; its squares hosted military parades and royal ceremonies.
Gives onto the open Mechouar grounds before the royal palace of Fes el-Jdid.
Kept its ceremonial role into the modern era as a threshold of the makhzen.
legend: They say the corpse of the captive Portuguese prince Ferdinand was hung from these walls for four days in 1443.
w: A great gaunt gate between the two parade grounds of the Mechouar, where sultans staged ceremonies and, when it suited them, public horrors. They say the captive Portuguese prince Ferdinand, dead in a Fes dungeon in 1443, was hung here naked for four days. Power, performed in stone.

hook: A sultan's ruined arms factory of the 1880s, built by Italian engineers to make rifles.
facts: Established by Sultan Moulay Hassan in 1885-86 with Italian officers, west of the royal palace.
Sited by the Oued Fes so the flowing water could power its forges and machinery.
Began production around 1889, turning out up to five rifles a day before fully running by 1891.
Shut under the French Protectorate in 1912, later used for rugs, now largely abandoned.
w: A sultan's bid to drag Morocco into the industrial age: forges and lathes set by the Oued Fes so the river could turn them, run by imported Italian engineers, churning out five rifles a day. The French shut it in 1912. Now vaulted halls and the grand Italianate Bab Makina stand half-ruined. Modernity, rusted.

hook: A Saadian hilltop fort with the city's broadest view across the whole medina basin.
facts: Built in 1582 by the Saadian sultan Ahmad al-Mansur to guard and overawe the city.
One of a pair with Borj Nord, modelled on Portuguese bastion design.
Likely raised using European captives taken at the 1578 Battle of the Three Kings.
Stands on a hill south of the medina with a near-360-degree panorama.
w: The sultan who built this in 1582 pointed its cannon at his own city as much as at any enemy, a fortress to overawe Fes from the southern hills. Likely raised by Portuguese captives from the Battle of the Three Kings. Climb up at dusk and the whole medina spreads below, smoking and ringing. The best view in town.
